articles.headline: DOJ Releases Withheld Epstein Files Containing Assault Accusations Against Trump
articles.subheadline: Three FBI interview memos were 'incorrectly coded as duplicative,' the Justice Department said, after Democrats alleged a cover-up.
articles.body:
The U.S. Department of Justice has published previously withheld documents from the Jeffrey Epstein investigation that include FBI summaries of interviews with an unnamed woman who accused both Epstein and President Donald Trump of sexually assaulting her when she was a teenager in the 1980s. The White House called the allegations "completely baseless" and "backed by zero credible evidence."

The three newly released memos, totaling more than 50 pages, document a series of FBI interviews conducted in 2019 with the woman as part of the federal investigation into Epstein's accomplice Ghislaine Maxwell, who was convicted and sentenced in 2022 for sex trafficking. According to the files, the woman told agents she was introduced to Trump by Epstein when she was a teenager and alleged both men assaulted her between the ages of 13 and 15. The FBI had no further contact with the woman after those interviews, the documents state. It remains unclear whether Trump and Epstein knew each other during the time period the woman described.

The documents' absence from an earlier mass release of Epstein files had drawn sharp criticism from congressional Democrats, who accused the Justice Department of orchestrating a cover-up to protect the president. NPR first identified the gap by cross-referencing index numbers and serial codes in the published files. The DOJ attributed the omission to a clerical error, saying the memos had been "incorrectly coded as duplicative" during the review process. White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said Trump had been "totally exonerated" by the broader Epstein file releases and noted that the Biden-era Justice Department had not pursued charges based on the claims.

The files are part of millions of documents the DOJ has been compelled to release under a law Congress passed in November requiring disclosure of all material from federal Epstein investigations. Beyond the interview memos, the broader trove includes tips submitted to the FBI's national Threat Operation Center containing numerous unverified allegations of sexual abuse against Trump, Epstein, and other prominent figures. The DOJ has previously cautioned that some documents contain "untrue and sensationalist claims" submitted shortly before the 2020 election. Trump has not been accused of any crimes by Epstein survivors who have come forward publicly.

The controversy has escalated on Capitol Hill, where the House Oversight Committee — in a bipartisan vote that included Republicans — approved a subpoena for Attorney General Pam Bondi to testify about the department's handling of the Epstein records. The move underscores growing congressional impatience with the pace and transparency of the document releases, even as the DOJ insists the withholding was inadvertent rather than deliberate.

articles.headline: Solar Rotation Theory Upended After 45 Years of Assumptions
articles.subheadline: Nagoya University simulations show sun-like stars keep the same spin pattern for life, contradicting decades of predictions
articles.body:
A team at Nagoya University in Japan has overturned a foundational assumption in stellar physics, demonstrating through the most detailed interior simulations ever conducted that sun-like stars do not switch their rotation patterns as they age. The finding, published in Nature Astronomy, contradicts a theoretical prediction that has stood largely unchallenged for 45 years.

Since the early 1980s, astrophysicists have widely accepted that stars similar to our sun undergo a dramatic rotational transition over their lifetimes. The prevailing model held that young stars spin fastest at their equators and slowest at their poles — a pattern observed in our own sun — but that aging stars eventually flip this arrangement, with the poles accelerating to rotate faster than the equator. This so-called "anti-solar" rotation was considered a natural consequence of how convective flows inside a star interact with its decreasing spin rate over billions of years.

The Nagoya researchers ran high-resolution simulations of stellar interiors that captured the complex interplay of turbulent convection, magnetic fields, and rotational dynamics with unprecedented fidelity. Their results showed no evidence of the predicted transition. Instead, stars like the sun appear to maintain their equator-fast, pole-slow rotation — known as solar-type differential rotation — throughout their entire main-sequence lifetime.

The implications ripple across multiple areas of astrophysics. Differential rotation is a key driver of stellar magnetic activity, which in turn governs phenomena ranging from starspot cycles to coronal mass ejections. For exoplanet scientists, the magnetic behavior of host stars directly influences assessments of planetary habitability. If older stars do not transition to anti-solar rotation, models of stellar magnetism and space weather around aging systems may need significant revision.

The result also highlights the growing power of computational astrophysics to test long-held theoretical assumptions. While observational evidence for anti-solar rotation in sun-like stars has always been thin, the prediction persisted in textbooks and models largely because no simulation had been detailed enough to rigorously challenge it. The Nagoya team's work suggests that what astronomers see in our own middle-aged sun — a star spinning fastest at its midsection — may simply be the permanent state of affairs for solar-type stars from birth to death.

articles.headline: Physicists Confirm 50-Year-Old Theory of 2D Magnetic Vortices
articles.subheadline: UT Austin team observes full sequence of exotic magnetic phases in atom-thin material, validating the six-state clock model
articles.body:
A team of physicists at the University of Texas at Austin has experimentally confirmed a theoretical prediction first made in the 1970s, directly observing nanoscale magnetic vortices forming in an atomically thin material. The results, published in Nature Materials in March 2026, validate a foundational model of two-dimensional magnetism and could open pathways toward ultracompact magnetic technologies.

The researchers cooled a single-atom-thick crystal of nickel phosphorus trisulfide (NiPS₃) to temperatures between roughly −150°C and −130°C, at which point the material entered what is known as a Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless (BKT) phase. In this state, the magnetic moments of individual atoms spontaneously organized into swirling vortex pairs — one spinning clockwise, the other counterclockwise — tightly bound together and spanning just a few nanometers in width. The BKT phase transition is named for Vadim Berezinskii and Nobel laureates J. Michael Kosterlitz and David Thouless, who shared the 2016 Nobel Prize in Physics for their theoretical framework describing such topological phase transitions.

As the material was cooled further, it transitioned into a second distinct magnetic state called a six-state clock ordered phase, in which magnetic moments snapped into one of six symmetrically related directions. While previous experiments had detected each of these phases individually in different systems, this marks the first time the complete sequence — from disordered state to BKT vortex phase to clock-ordered phase — has been observed unfolding within a single material. That full sequence is precisely what the two-dimensional six-state clock model, proposed roughly half a century ago, predicted should occur.

"The BKT phase is particularly intriguing because these vortices are predicted to be exceptionally robust and confined to just a few nanometers laterally while occupying only a single atomic layer in thickness," said Edoardo Baldini, an assistant professor of physics at UT Austin who led the research. He noted that the vortices' stability and minuscule size offer a novel route to controlling magnetism at the nanoscale, with implications for both fundamental topological physics and future device concepts.

The study was co-led by senior authors Allan MacDonald and Xiaoqin "Elaine" Li, both UT physicists and members of the Texas Quantum Institute, with co-first authors Frank Y. Gao and Dong Seob Kim. Funding came from the National Science Foundation, the Robert A. Welch Foundation, the W. M. Keck Foundation, and several federal research offices. The team now aims to find materials that can sustain these exotic magnetic phases at higher temperatures, potentially approaching room temperature — a milestone that could transform nanoscale magnetic vortices from a laboratory curiosity into the basis of practical spintronic and electronic devices.

articles.headline: TerraPower Wins First NRC Nuclear Reactor Permit in Nearly a Decade
articles.subheadline: Bill Gates-founded startup will build a sodium-cooled Natrium reactor at a Wyoming coal plant site, marking a milestone for advanced nuclear
articles.body:
The Nuclear Regulatory Commission has granted TerraPower permission to construct a new nuclear reactor in Wyoming — the first such permit the agency has issued in nearly ten years and a significant milestone for the advanced nuclear energy sector.

The approval, announced this week, authorizes TerraPower to build its Natrium reactor on private land adjacent to an aging coal power plant. Founded by Bill Gates in 2015 and backed by investors including Nvidia, the company designed the 345-megawatt reactor in partnership with GE Vernova Hitachi. The plant sits in an unusual middle ground: roughly two-thirds smaller than conventional full-size reactors, but substantially larger than the small modular reactor designs pursued by many competitors.

What sets Natrium apart most fundamentally is its cooling system. Rather than the pressurized water used in virtually every commercial reactor built over the past half-century, TerraPower's design relies on molten sodium — making it the first non-water-cooled commercial reactor approved by the NRC in more than 40 years. The company says molten sodium cooling offers safety advantages and a built-in energy storage capability: excess heat generated during periods of low electricity demand can be banked in large insulated tanks of molten sodium, then dispatched when wind and solar output drops. This thermal battery feature could help nuclear compete more effectively in grids with high renewable penetration by allowing the reactor to run at full capacity continuously while still matching variable demand.

Notably, TerraPower navigated the NRC's traditional permitting pathway for construction on private property, rather than relying on recently loosened Department of Energy safety rules that apply only to federally owned land. The decision to pursue the established regulatory route may lend the project additional credibility as the company moves toward construction.

TerraPower is among a growing cohort of nuclear startups attracting substantial venture and corporate capital, driven by surging electricity demand from data centers and broader political support for nuclear expansion under the Trump administration. Investors have poured well over $1 billion into nuclear startups in recent months. TerraPower itself has raised $1.7 billion to date, including a $650 million round that closed in June 2025, according to PitchBook. Yet the road ahead remains steep. Nuclear energy has historically been among the most expensive forms of new generating capacity, plagued by cost overruns, while solar, wind, and battery storage have seen dramatic price declines. Startups like TerraPower are betting that mass manufacturing techniques can eventually bring nuclear costs down — a theory that, by the industry's own admission, could take a decade or more to validate.

articles.headline: Strait of Hormuz Shipping Grinds to Near Halt for Sixth Day
articles.subheadline: Only Iran-linked tankers are transiting the critical oil chokepoint as commercial vessels stay away
articles.body:
Commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has remained at a virtual standstill for a sixth consecutive day, with only tankers linked to Iran making the passage through the world's most critical oil chokepoint, according to tracking data reported by Bloomberg.

The prolonged disruption marks one of the most significant interruptions to traffic through the narrow waterway in recent memory. The strait, which separates Iran from Oman and the United Arab Emirates, typically handles roughly one-fifth of the world's daily oil consumption — approximately 20 million barrels per day — along with substantial liquefied natural gas shipments from Qatar and other Gulf producers.

Over the past 24 hours, Iran-linked vessels have been the only large ships recorded transiting the passage, while other commercial operators continue to avoid the route. The nature of the standoff underscores the outsized leverage that tensions in the region can exert on global energy markets. Any sustained closure or avoidance of Hormuz has long been considered one of the most destabilizing scenarios for global oil supply, with limited alternative pipeline capacity available to reroute Gulf crude exports.

The six-day disruption raises acute questions for energy traders and importing nations alike. Countries including Japan, South Korea, India, and China rely heavily on crude and LNG cargoes that pass through the strait. Extended avoidance by commercial tankers could tighten global supply chains and put upward pressure on energy prices, even if physical volumes from Iran-linked ships continue to flow.

Analysts have long warned that the Strait of Hormuz represents a single point of failure for global energy security. While Saudi Arabia maintains some overland pipeline capacity to terminals on the Red Sea, and the UAE operates a pipeline bypassing the strait to the port of Fujairah, these alternatives can handle only a fraction of normal Hormuz volumes. The longer commercial vessels stay away, the greater the risk of tangible supply disruptions rippling through global markets.

articles.headline: BlackRock Limits Withdrawals on $26B Credit Fund, Sparking Crypto Fears
articles.subheadline: Growing stress in the $3.5 trillion private credit market threatens digital assets through both macro contagion and tokenized lending channels
articles.body:
BlackRock has begun restricting redemptions from its $26 billion private credit fund amid surging withdrawal requests, marking the latest sign of deepening stress in a sector that experts warn could send shockwaves through cryptocurrency and decentralized finance markets.

The move, reported by Bloomberg on Friday, follows turbulence at rival Blue Owl Capital, which sold $1.4 billion in loans last month to meet its own redemption demands and reportedly holds exposure to a collapsed U.K. property lender. Shares of major alternative asset managers — including BlackRock, Apollo Global Management, Ares Management, and KKR — dropped between 4% and 6% on Friday, deepening what has become a painful 2026 selloff for the sector. The private credit market, estimated at roughly $3.5 trillion globally by the Alternative Credit Council, has become a focal point of investor anxiety as economic conditions deteriorate.

The potential transmission mechanism to crypto markets runs through two distinct channels. The first is macroeconomic contagion: U.S. banks had extended nearly $300 billion in loans to private credit providers and another $285 billion to private equity funds as of mid-2025, according to Andreja Cobeljic, head of derivatives trading at Swiss crypto bank AMINA Bank. A forced unwinding of positions could trigger cascading deleveraging across risk assets, including bitcoin, which slipped below $68,000 over the weekend. "In isolation this would be manageable," Cobeljic wrote in a note to clients. "But emerging in the middle of a broader global deleveraging event, alongside an energy shock and collapsing rate-cut expectations, it is a different conversation."

The second channel is more direct and novel. Tokenized private credit products — traditional loans packaged as blockchain-based tokens — have grown to nearly $5 billion on-chain, according to data from rwa.xyz. While still a fraction of the broader market, these instruments are increasingly embedded in DeFi protocols as collateral for borrowing. A 2025 episode illustrated the risk clearly: when auto-parts supplier First Brands Group filed for bankruptcy, a tokenized credit strategy on the Midas RWA platform saw its net asset value decline roughly 2%, pushing leveraged borrowers on the Morpho lending protocol toward liquidation. Lenders avoided losses, but the incident demonstrated how off-chain credit deterioration can propagate directly into on-chain markets.

"Institutions are entering crypto, but often with products that even degens and DeFi natives don't fully grasp," said Teddy Pornprinya, co-founder of real-world asset protocol Plume, warning that headline yields on tokenized credit products can obscure underlying fee structures and credit risks. With bitcoin already under pressure — on-chain data from Glassnode shows approximately 43% of the supply now sitting at a loss — a disorderly unwind in private credit could deliver what Cobeljic called "a significant second-order shock that current pricing does not reflect."

articles.headline: Ancient Sheep Bone Yields First Non-Human Bronze Age Plague DNA
articles.subheadline: Discovery at a 4,000-year-old Ural Mountains settlement suggests livestock helped spread plague millennia before the Black Death
articles.body:
Scientists have identified the bacterium *Yersinia pestis* in the remains of a domesticated sheep that lived roughly 4,000 years ago — the first time the plague pathogen has been detected in a non-human host from the Bronze Age. The discovery, published in the journal Cell, offers a critical clue to one of archaeology's most persistent puzzles: how an early form of plague spread across Eurasia long before fleas and rats fueled the medieval Black Death.

The infected sheep bone was excavated from Arkaim, a fortified settlement in Russia's Southern Ural Mountains associated with the Sintashta culture, known for early horse riding, advanced bronze weaponry, and extensive migration across Central Asia. An international team led by researchers from Harvard University, the University of Arkansas, and institutions in Germany, Russia, and South Korea extracted and sequenced ancient *Y. pestis* DNA from the specimen, confirming a lineage distinct from the flea-adapted strain that would later kill roughly a third of Europe's population.

The finding matters because the Bronze Age strain of plague lacked the genetic machinery to spread via flea bites — the transmission route that made the 14th-century Black Death so lethal. Scientists had long struggled to explain how identical plague strains could appear in human remains separated by thousands of kilometers. "It had to be more than people moving," said Taylor Hermes, an archaeologist at the University of Arkansas who co-leads the ancient livestock DNA project. "We now see it as a dynamic between people, livestock, and some still-unidentified natural reservoir — which could be rodents on the Eurasian steppe or migratory birds."

Hermes's team typically analyzes genetic material from ancient bones and teeth to trace how domesticated animals — cattle, goats, and sheep — migrated out of the Fertile Crescent and shaped early nomadic cultures. Working with such degraded material is painstaking: recovered DNA fragments often measure only about 50 base pairs, compared with the more than 3 billion in a complete human genome, and animal remains tend to be far less well preserved than carefully buried human bones. The plague genome emerged unexpectedly from what Hermes described as a "complex genetic soup of contamination."

The researchers argue that the Bronze Age expansion of livestock herding, combined with increased horse-borne mobility across the steppe, likely brought human populations into closer and more frequent contact with environmental disease reservoirs. Hermes recently secured a five-year, 100,000-euro grant from Germany's Max Planck Society to continue excavations near Arkaim in search of additional infected remains. He drew a direct line to modern public health: expanding economic activity into undisturbed ecosystems, he cautioned, risks triggering the same kind of disease spillover that appears to have shaped Bronze Age societies thousands of years ago.

articles.headline: ALMA Delivers Most Detailed Map of the Milky Way's Chaotic Center
articles.subheadline: Largest-ever ALMA mosaic reveals 650 light-years of cold gas filaments fueling extreme star formation near our galaxy's black hole
articles.body:
Astronomers using the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array have produced the most detailed image ever captured of the Milky Way's turbulent core, revealing an intricate web of cold gas filaments threading through the region surrounding our galaxy's central supermassive black hole. The mosaic, spanning 650 light-years across and stretching the equivalent of three full Moons in the sky, represents the largest single dataset ALMA has ever assembled.

The image is the product of the ALMA CMZ Exploration Survey, or ACES, an ambitious international collaboration involving more than 160 scientists from over 70 institutions worldwide. Led by Steven Longmore, a professor of astrophysics at Liverpool John Moores University in the United Kingdom, the project mapped the Central Molecular Zone — the dense concentration of gas and dust encircling the Milky Way's center — with a level of precision never before achieved. The survey detected dozens of distinct molecules in the region, ranging from simple compounds like silicon monoxide to complex organic molecules including methanol, acetone, and ethanol, pointing to a far richer chemical environment than previously understood.

The Central Molecular Zone is home to some of the most massive and short-lived stars in the galaxy, many of which end their lives in violent supernova or even hypernova explosions. Understanding how stars form under such extreme conditions — where intense radiation, powerful magnetic fields, and gravitational forces from the supermassive black hole all compete — has long challenged astrophysicists. The ACES data show cold molecular gas streaming along elongated filaments that channel material into dense clumps, the seeds of future stars. While this filamentary process is well-documented in calmer regions of the galaxy, observing it in the galactic center's hostile environment could force revisions to existing models of star formation.

Longmore noted that the Central Molecular Zone may serve as a local laboratory for understanding the early universe. "We believe the region shares many features with galaxies in the early Universe, where stars were forming in chaotic, extreme environments," he said. By studying stellar birth in these conditions, researchers hope to reconstruct how entire galaxies grew and evolved billions of years ago.

The results are described across six papers accepted or under review at Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society, with the full dataset to be released through the ALMA Science Portal. The team expects even deeper insights to come. Ashley Barnes, an astronomer at the European Southern Observatory and a co-principal investigator on the project, pointed to the forthcoming ALMA Wideband Sensitivity Upgrade and ESO's Extremely Large Telescope as tools that will push resolution further, tracing finer structures and more complex chemistry in the galaxy's most extreme neighborhood. "In many ways," Barnes said, "this is just the beginning."

articles.headline: Two Genes Hold the Key to Reviving Exhausted Cancer-Fighting T Cells
articles.subheadline: A new genetic atlas of immune cell states reveals molecular switches that restore tumor-killing power without sacrificing long-term immunity
articles.body:
A multi-institution research team has identified two previously unknown genes whose suppression can restore the cancer-killing function of worn-out immune cells — a discovery that could reshape the future of immunotherapy for solid tumors and chronic infections.

Published March 5 in Nature, the study by scientists at the Salk Institute for Biological Studies, UNC Lineberger Comprehensive Cancer Center, and UC San Diego details the construction of a comprehensive genetic atlas mapping nine distinct states of CD8 "killer" T cells. These cells are the immune system's primary weapon against virus-infected and cancerous cells, but they progressively lose effectiveness when confronted with persistent threats such as tumors — a deterioration known as T cell exhaustion. The atlas allowed researchers to trace the molecular spectrum from fully protective to severely impaired, revealing transcription factors that act as fate-determining switches. Among them, two regulators called ZSCAN20 and JDP2, never before linked to T cell exhaustion, stood out. When the team disabled both genes, exhausted T cells regained their ability to destroy tumors while retaining long-term immune memory — a dual outcome that had long been considered difficult, if not impossible, to achieve simultaneously.

"We flipped specific genetic switches in the T cells to see if we could restore their tumor-killing function without damaging their ability to provide long-term immune protection," said co-corresponding author H. Kay Chung, an assistant professor at UNC Lineberger who began the work at Salk. "We found that it was indeed possible to separate these two outcomes." The finding challenges a longstanding assumption in immunology that exhaustion is an irreversible consequence of sustained immune activation.

The implications extend directly to clinical therapies. Adoptive cell transfer and CAR T cell therapy — treatments in which a patient's own immune cells are engineered and reinfused — have shown remarkable success in blood cancers but frequently falter against solid tumors, where the immunosuppressive microenvironment drives T cell burnout. The new atlas could serve as a recipe book for programming more durable therapeutic cells. "By separating these two programs, we can begin to design immune cells that are both durable and effective in cancer and chronic infection," said co-corresponding author Susan Kaech, a professor at Salk at the time of the study.

Looking ahead, the collaboration plans to integrate AI-guided computational modeling with experimental techniques to identify additional genetic combinations that can steer T cells into desired functional states. Co-corresponding author Wei Wang of UC San Diego noted that because genes operate in complex regulatory networks, powerful computational tools are essential for pinpointing the regulators that drive specific cell fates. The research was supported by multiple National Institutes of Health grants and the Damon Runyon Cancer Research Foundation.

articles.headline: Middle East Conflict Sends Economic Ripple Effects Across Global Markets
articles.subheadline: Escalating regional hostilities are straining supply chains, energy prices, and trade routes far beyond the war zone
articles.body:
The intensifying conflict in the Middle East is generating economic consequences that now extend well beyond the region's borders, threatening to complicate an already fragile global recovery.

According to reporting from Bloomberg, the war's fallout is widening as disruptions to critical trade corridors and energy infrastructure ripple through international markets. The Middle East remains a linchpin of global commerce — home to roughly a third of the world's seaborne oil shipments and key shipping lanes including the Strait of Hormuz and the Suez Canal corridor. Any sustained escalation raises the specter of higher energy costs, strained supply chains, and renewed inflationary pressures at a time when central banks in major economies have been cautiously easing monetary policy.

The strains come at a particularly vulnerable moment for the global economy. Growth prospects in Europe and parts of Asia remain tepid, and policymakers have limited fiscal room to absorb new shocks. Rising shipping and insurance costs tied to regional instability have already begun filtering into freight markets, echoing the disruptions seen during earlier rounds of Houthi attacks on Red Sea commercial vessels. Economists have warned that a broader conflict drawing in additional state actors could push oil prices significantly higher, adding percentage points to headline inflation in import-dependent nations.

For emerging markets and developing economies, the consequences may be especially acute. Many are net energy importers with thin foreign exchange reserves, leaving them exposed to commodity price swings. A prolonged disruption could also dampen investor sentiment toward the broader region, reducing capital flows at a time when several Gulf states are pursuing ambitious economic diversification programs.

Global policymakers and multilateral institutions are watching the situation closely. The International Monetary Fund and World Bank have repeatedly flagged geopolitical risk as a top threat to the economic outlook, and the latest escalation underscores just how quickly regional conflicts can metastasize into systemic economic challenges. With diplomatic efforts so far failing to produce a durable ceasefire, markets appear to be pricing in a prolonged period of elevated uncertainty.

articles.headline: Nintendo Sues U.S. Government Seeking Refund on IEEPA Tariffs
articles.subheadline: The gaming giant joins over a thousand companies demanding duties back after Supreme Court struck down Trump's tariff authority
articles.body:
Nintendo has filed a lawsuit against the United States government in the U.S. Court of International Trade, seeking reimbursement for tariff duties the company paid under President Donald Trump's executive orders invoking the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). The suit, filed on Friday, adds one of the world's most recognizable consumer electronics brands to a growing wave of corporate plaintiffs challenging the legality of the levies.

The Japanese gaming company's complaint comes in the wake of a Supreme Court ruling that determined Trump exceeded his presidential authority in imposing tariffs under IEEPA. According to the filing, reviewed by TechCrunch, the tariffs collectively generated more than $200 billion in duties on imports — a staggering sum that companies across industries are now racing to reclaim. More than a thousand businesses have already filed similar refund lawsuits.

"We can confirm that we have filed a request," Nintendo said in a brief statement. "We have nothing else to share on the topic." The company, which manufactures its consoles and hardware primarily in Asia, would have faced significant exposure to the import duties on goods entering the U.S. market.

The legal landscape around the tariffs continues to escalate. In response to the Supreme Court decision — which Trump publicly labeled "extraordinarily anti-American" — the president raised tariff rates from 10% to 15%. That move has prompted 24 states to file their own legal challenges, arguing that the increase represents yet another overreach of executive power. The cascading lawsuits suggest the tariff dispute is far from resolved, with the federal judiciary likely to remain a central battleground.

For Nintendo, the timing is notable. The company is in a critical commercial period as it continues to build momentum around its latest hardware cycle. The added cost burden of tariffs — and the uncertainty over whether refunds will ultimately be granted — introduces a material financial variable for a company that ships millions of units into the American market annually. The outcome of these cases could reshape how the executive branch wields trade policy tools for years to come.

articles.headline: Soham Murderer Ian Huntley Dies After Prison Attack
articles.subheadline: The convicted child killer, 52, had been on life support since suffering severe head trauma in a February assault at HMP Frankland
articles.body:
Ian Huntley, the former school caretaker convicted of murdering 10-year-old schoolgirls Holly Wells and Jessica Chapman in 2002, died on Saturday after weeks on life support following a violent attack by a fellow inmate at a high-security prison in northern England.

Huntley, 52, suffered significant head trauma on February 26 when he was bludgeoned with a makeshift weapon at a workshop inside HMP Frankland in County Durham. He was found lying in a pool of blood and transferred to hospital, where he remained on life support until it was switched off on Friday. Triple killer Anthony Russell, 43, is the suspected assailant, according to sources familiar with the investigation. Durham Constabulary confirmed that a police investigation is ongoing and that officers are preparing a file for the Crown Prosecution Service to consider charges.

The UK Ministry of Justice acknowledged Huntley's death, stating that his crime "remains one of the most shocking and devastating cases in our nation's history" and extending thoughts to the victims' families. Huntley had been a frequent target of violence during his incarceration, surviving multiple previous attacks over his two decades behind bars.

The case that led to Huntley's life sentence remains seared into British collective memory. In August 2002, Holly Wells and Jessica Chapman — best friends photographed in matching red Manchester United shirts — vanished after leaving a family barbecue in the quiet Cambridgeshire town of Soham. They were believed to be walking to buy sweets when Huntley, then 28 and working as a caretaker at a local school, lured them to his home and killed them. Their disappearance triggered a massive search effort and dominated national headlines for two weeks before their bodies were discovered in a ditch near RAF Lakenheath in Suffolk, roughly 10 miles from Soham. Huntley was arrested the same day.

He was convicted and sentenced to life imprisonment with a minimum term of 40 years, meaning he would not have been eligible for parole until 2042. His then-girlfriend Maxine Carr was separately convicted of conspiring to pervert the course of justice for providing a false alibi and was jailed in 2003; she has since been released. The case prompted a sweeping review of police vetting procedures across England, after it emerged that Huntley had previously come to the attention of authorities over allegations involving underage girls before securing his position at the school.

articles.headline: Emirates Resumes Flights After Missile Threat Forces Brief Suspension
articles.subheadline: Dubai's flagship carrier temporarily halted operations Saturday amid regional missile interceptions
articles.body:
Emirates, Dubai's flagship airline, announced plans to resume flight operations on Saturday after a temporary suspension triggered by missile threats in the region, marking the latest in a series of disruptions linked to ongoing military activity.

The carrier halted services briefly after another round of missile interceptions raised safety concerns for commercial aviation in and around the Gulf region. The suspension, though short-lived, underscores the persistent vulnerability of one of the world's busiest aviation hubs to regional security flare-ups. Dubai International Airport serves as a critical global transit point, handling tens of millions of passengers annually and serving as the home base for Emirates' vast long-haul network.

The latest disruption follows previous incidents in which Emirates and other Gulf-based carriers were forced to pause or reroute flights due to missile and drone threats. Such interruptions, even when brief, carry significant logistical and financial consequences for airlines operating high-frequency schedules across multiple continents. Emirates operates one of the largest wide-body fleets in the world, and even short groundings can cascade into delays affecting thousands of passengers.

The resumption of flights suggests that authorities determined the immediate threat had passed, though the recurring nature of these suspensions raises broader questions about the stability of air corridors in the Middle East. Aviation regulators and airlines have developed increasingly rapid response protocols for such events, balancing passenger safety with the economic imperative to minimize downtime.

For Dubai, which has staked much of its economic identity on its role as a global aviation and tourism crossroads, repeated flight disruptions represent a strategic concern that extends well beyond any single airline. The emirate's leadership has invested heavily in positioning Dubai as a safe and reliable hub, and sustained instability in regional airspace could test that reputation over time.

articles.headline: Armed Ground Robots Enter Combat in Ukraine, Heralding New Era of Warfare
articles.subheadline: Ukrainian forces deploy world's first UGV battalion as both sides race to field killer robots amid severe manpower shortages
articles.body:
Armed robotic ground vehicles are now actively fighting on the battlefields of Ukraine, marking what military officials and manufacturers describe as the dawn of ground-based robot warfare — a development with profound implications for the future of armed conflict.

Ukraine's K2 brigade has established what its commander, Major Oleksandr Afanasiev, calls the world's first uncrewed ground vehicle (UGV) battalion. The unit deploys robots mounted with Kalashnikov machine guns and grenade launchers, as well as battery-powered kamikaze vehicles designed to silently destroy enemy positions. Unlike the aerial drones that have defined much of the war's technological character, these ground robots approach targets without the telltale buzzing that warns of an incoming strike. Reports from the front indicate UGVs have successfully repelled Russian assaults, ambushed armored personnel carriers, and even forced enemy soldiers to surrender — with at least one incident involving Russian and Ukrainian robots clashing without any humans present at the site of battle.

The rapid deployment is driven by grim arithmetic. Aerial drones have expanded Ukraine's so-called "kill zone" to 20–25 kilometers from the line of contact, making it extraordinarily dangerous for infantry to operate. "Ukraine can afford to lose robots, but it simply cannot afford to lose battle-ready soldiers," Afanasiev said, referencing the severe manpower shortages that have plagued Ukrainian forces. Valerii Zaluzhnyi, Ukraine's former commander-in-chief and current ambassador to the United Kingdom, told a Chatham House audience that strike UGVs would soon operate as part of large, AI-powered swarms attacking simultaneously from air, ground, and sea.

The industrial scale of production is accelerating dramatically. Ukrainian manufacturer Tencore produced more than 2,000 UGVs in 2025, and its director, Maksym Vasylchenko, projects demand will surge to approximately 40,000 units in 2026, with 10–15 percent armed with weapons. Devdroid, another Ukrainian firm, delivered hundreds of "strike droids" last year and is developing systems that would allow robots to autonomously navigate to a location, carry out surveillance or combat tasks, and return to base independently. Russia, too, is fielding its own combat robots, including the Kuryer — capable of mounting a flamethrower or heavy machine gun and operating autonomously for five hours — and the Lyagushka kamikaze vehicle.

Despite the technological leap, both sides maintain that humans retain authority over lethal decisions. A deputy commander from Ukraine's 33rd Mechanised Brigade, identified by the callsign Afghan, emphasized that current UGVs are only semi-autonomous: they can move, observe, and detect enemies independently, but firing requires human authorization. "Robots can misidentify the wrong person or attack a civilian. That's why the final decision must be made by an operator," he said — a self-imposed constraint rooted in international humanitarian law. Yet as the volume and sophistication of these machines grow, the ethical and strategic questions surrounding autonomous weapons are shifting rapidly from theoretical debate to battlefield reality.

articles.headline: Trump Demands Role in Choosing Iran's Next Leader After Khamenei Assassination
articles.subheadline: U.S. president calls for 'unconditional surrender' and says Khamenei's son is 'unacceptable,' drawing sharp rebuke from Tehran
articles.body:
President Donald Trump has injected himself directly into the question of who will succeed Iran's assassinated Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, declaring he must be "involved in the appointment" and demanding Iran's "unconditional surrender" before any new leader is installed — a posture that Tehran's interim leadership has furiously rejected.

One week into the U.S.-Israeli military campaign that killed Khamenei and dozens of senior clerics and commanders, Trump told Axios and Reuters that he would only accept "a GREAT AND ACCEPTABLE leader" for Iran, explicitly ruling out 56-year-old Mojtaba Khamenei, the late supreme leader's son and a figure widely considered the front-runner for succession. Trump drew a parallel to Venezuela, where U.S. forces extracted President Nicolás Maduro on January 3 and installed Delcy Rodriguez, who has since cooperated with Washington. "I have to be involved in the appointment, like with Delcy in Venezuela," he said, calling that operation the "perfect scenario" for Iran.

The comparison, however, strains against the realities of Iran's political architecture. The Islamic Republic's overlapping layers of clerical, political, and security institutions — refined over four decades of external pressure and internal unrest — bear little resemblance to Venezuela's government. Iran's collective memory of the CIA- and MI6-backed 1953 coup that overthrew Prime Minister Mohammad Mosaddegh remains a foundational grievance, making any perception of foreign interference in leadership selection politically radioactive. Tehran's conservative Mehr News Agency carried a sharp rebuke from the interim Leadership Council: "The great Iranian nation will never allow anyone to interfere in its internal affairs and its right to determine its own destiny."

Behind the defiant rhetoric, Iran's succession process is unfolding under extraordinary duress. The Assembly of Experts — the 88-member clerical body responsible for selecting a supreme leader — has reportedly been meeting virtually after its usual venues in Tehran and Qom were bombed. Reports conflict on whether Mojtaba Khamenei has already been anointed. Mehdi Khalaji, a senior fellow at the Washington Institute for Near East Policy, told the BBC he doubts the succession has been finalized, noting the interim council's recent assertion of authority over war-and-peace decisions suggests the process remains incomplete. Other names in contention include Hassan Khomeini, grandson of the republic's founder and considered a relative moderate, and senior jurist Alireza Arafi, currently serving on the interim Leadership Council. The assembly may also opt for a leadership council rather than a single successor.

The selection carries mortal stakes: Israel has declared that any new supreme leader will be an "unequivocal target." Trump himself acknowledged the grim calculus with characteristic bluntness: "Most of the people we had in mind are dead." His stated goal appears to be not regime change but a change within the regime — a distinction likely to disappoint Iranian dissidents who hoped Khamenei's death would spell the end of the Islamic Republic itself. Whether Iran's fractured but defiant establishment will bend to external pressure or rally around a hardline successor remains the central question of a conflict with no clear endgame in sight.

articles.headline: Saudi Arabia Shoots Down Drones Targeting Major Shaybah Oil Field
articles.subheadline: The remote desert facility can produce up to 1 million barrels of crude per day, underscoring ongoing security risks to global energy supply
articles.body:
Saudi Arabia's air defenses intercepted multiple drones over the Empty Quarter desert that were on course toward the Shaybah oil field, one of the kingdom's most strategically important energy installations, the state-run Saudi Press Agency reported on March 7.

The Shaybah complex, operated by Saudi Aramco deep in the Rub' al-Khali desert near the border with the United Arab Emirates, has production capacity of approximately 1 million barrels of crude oil per day. An attack on the facility could have significant consequences for global oil markets, which remain sensitive to any disruption in Middle Eastern supply. No damage to the field's infrastructure was reported following the interception.

The incident recalls a pattern of aerial threats to Saudi energy assets that has persisted for years. In September 2019, a devastating drone and missile strike on Aramco's Abqaiq processing facility and the Khurais oil field temporarily knocked out roughly half of the kingdom's production — some 5.7 million barrels per day — and sent oil prices surging by nearly 15 percent in a single session. Since then, Riyadh has invested heavily in layered air defense systems, including upgrades to radar coverage over remote installations like Shaybah.

No group immediately claimed responsibility for the latest attempted attack. Yemen's Houthi movement has repeatedly targeted Saudi oil infrastructure in recent years as part of the broader conflict on the Arabian Peninsula, though other non-state actors in the region have also demonstrated drone capabilities. The origin and type of the intercepted drones were not disclosed in the initial Saudi government statement.

Oil prices showed limited immediate reaction to the news, suggesting markets viewed the successful interception as evidence that Saudi defenses are functioning as intended. Still, the attempted strike serves as a reminder that the security premium embedded in crude prices reflects real and recurring threats to the world's most critical energy corridor. Analysts will be watching for any Saudi or coalition response, as well as further details on the provenance of the drones.

articles.headline: India Hikes Cooking Gas Prices Amid Strait of Hormuz Crisis
articles.subheadline: First LPG price increase in a year comes as Iran conflict disrupts Middle East energy flows and drives up global fuel costs
articles.body:
India has raised prices on liquefied petroleum gas, the cooking fuel used by hundreds of millions of its citizens, for the first time in over a year as the ongoing conflict involving Iran chokes energy shipments through the Strait of Hormuz and pushes global fuel costs sharply higher.

The price hike marks a significant policy shift for New Delhi, which had held LPG prices steady for more than twelve months — a period during which the government absorbed rising import costs rather than pass them on to consumers. The Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway between Iran and Oman through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil and a significant share of global LPG supplies transit daily, has become a critical chokepoint as the Iran war disrupts shipping routes and supply chains across the Persian Gulf region.

India is one of the world's largest importers of LPG, relying heavily on Middle Eastern suppliers to meet domestic demand. The fuel is a kitchen staple for a vast majority of Indian households, making its pricing an acutely sensitive political issue. Previous governments have faced public backlash over cooking gas price increases, and the decision to raise prices now signals the severity of the supply disruption and the fiscal pressure mounting on state-owned energy companies that manage LPG distribution.

The Hormuz crisis has sent ripples across global energy markets, with buyers in Asia scrambling to secure alternative supplies and freight costs surging as tankers reroute around conflict zones. For India, which imports a substantial portion of its LPG from Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and the United Arab Emirates — all of whose shipments transit the strait — the disruption poses both an economic and a humanitarian challenge, given the fuel's role in daily life for lower- and middle-income households.

Analysts expect further price pressures if the Hormuz disruption persists, potentially forcing New Delhi to expand subsidies or seek long-term supply agreements with non-Middle Eastern producers. The situation underscores the vulnerability of major energy-importing nations to geopolitical shocks in the Persian Gulf, a dynamic that has repeatedly shaped global energy policy over the past half-century.

articles.headline: Gold Sells at Rare Discount in Dubai as Middle East Conflict Disrupts Bullion Flows
articles.subheadline: Widening war grounds flights and strands inventories in one of the world's largest physical gold trading hubs
articles.body:
Gold is trading at an unusual discount in Dubai as an escalating Middle East conflict disrupts air freight routes and leaves significant bullion inventories stranded in the emirate, according to a report from Bloomberg on Thursday.

Dubai serves as one of the world's most critical nodes for physical gold trading, acting as a conduit between refineries, miners, and end markets across Asia, Europe, and Africa. The city's gold souk and free-trade zones handle hundreds of tons of bullion annually, making any disruption to flows out of the hub a significant event for global precious metals markets. The widening war has grounded flights and severely hampered suppliers' ability to move metal to buyers elsewhere, creating a localized glut that has forced sellers to offer gold below prevailing international benchmark prices — a rare inversion of normal market dynamics.

Under typical conditions, physical gold in Dubai trades at a modest premium to spot prices, reflecting the costs of refining, storage, and the emirate's role as a reliable distribution point. A discount signals that sellers are struggling to find routes to market and are competing aggressively to offload inventory to local or regional buyers who can take delivery without relying on disrupted logistics networks. The situation echoes, in reverse, the sharp premiums that emerged in certain markets during the early months of the COVID-19 pandemic, when supply-chain breakdowns created stark regional price dislocations.

The dislocation carries broader implications for the global gold market, which has already been shaped by geopolitical risk premiums over the past year. While benchmark gold futures have rallied as investors seek safe-haven assets amid the conflict, the physical market tells a more fractured story — with metal piling up in some locations even as shortages emerge in others. Refiners, banks, and logistics firms that depend on Dubai as a transit point may be forced to reroute shipments through alternative hubs, adding cost and complexity to an already strained supply chain.

Market participants will be watching closely to see whether the discount widens further or whether alternative freight corridors can be established to relieve the bottleneck. For now, the situation underscores how modern commodity markets, despite their vast scale, remain vulnerable to the physical realities of conflict and disrupted transportation networks.

articles.headline: Novo Nordisk and Hims & Hers Strike Deal to Sell Obesity Drugs Together
articles.subheadline: The partnership ends a bitter legal dispute between the pharmaceutical giant and the telehealth platform
articles.body:
Novo Nordisk, the Danish pharmaceutical giant behind blockbuster weight-loss medications, has agreed to sell its obesity drugs through Hims & Hers Health's telehealth platform, resolving a contentious public feud that had escalated into litigation just weeks ago.

The agreement, first reported by Bloomberg on March 7, marks a dramatic reversal in the relationship between the two companies. Novo Nordisk and Hims & Hers had been locked in an increasingly acrimonious dispute that culminated in legal action last month. The specifics of the settlement terms and the commercial arrangement have not been publicly disclosed, though a person familiar with the matter confirmed the partnership.

The deal represents a significant strategic shift for both companies. For Novo Nordisk, which manufactures the widely sought-after GLP-1 receptor agonists that have transformed the obesity treatment landscape, the Hims & Hers platform offers a direct-to-consumer digital channel that could expand access to its branded medications. For Hims & Hers, which has built a substantial business around telehealth consultations and prescription fulfillment, securing a direct supply relationship with Novo Nordisk provides legitimacy and a reliable source of the branded drugs that consumers have been clamoring for.

The feud between the two companies had played out against the backdrop of surging demand for weight-loss medications and a complex market that has included compounded versions of GLP-1 drugs. Hims & Hers had positioned itself as a major distributor in the obesity drug market, a stance that brought it into direct conflict with Novo Nordisk's efforts to control the distribution and branding of its products. The legal battle that erupted in February threatened to deepen that rift, making the swift pivot to partnership all the more striking.

The agreement could reshape how patients access obesity treatments in the United States, where demand for GLP-1 medications continues to outstrip supply through traditional pharmacy channels. By combining Novo Nordisk's pharmaceutical portfolio with Hims & Hers' digital health infrastructure, the partnership has the potential to streamline prescribing and delivery for millions of patients seeking weight-loss treatments. Investors and industry observers will be watching closely for details on pricing, exclusivity, and the scope of the collaboration.

articles.headline: U.S. Economy Sheds 92,000 Jobs in February, Defying Forecasts
articles.subheadline: Unexpected labor market contraction and rising unemployment revive speculation about Federal Reserve rate cuts in 2026
articles.body:
The U.S. labor market delivered a jarring surprise on Friday, with the economy shedding 92,000 jobs in February — a stark reversal from January's gain of 126,000 and a dramatic miss against economist forecasts calling for 59,000 new positions. The unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4%, above the expected 4.3%, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics.

The report marks the first significant contraction in nonfarm payrolls in months and immediately reshuffled market expectations around Federal Reserve policy. Prior to Friday's data, futures markets had priced in a 95% probability that the Fed would hold rates steady at its March 18 meeting and an 85% chance of no cut in April. The weak employment picture could now reopen the door to rate reductions in the first half of 2026, though the path forward remains complicated by competing inflationary pressures.

Financial markets reacted swiftly. U.S. stock index futures extended losses, with the Nasdaq falling 1% and the S&P 500 declining 0.8%. The 10-year Treasury yield dropped four basis points to 4.11%, reflecting a flight to safety and growing bets on easier monetary policy. Gold climbed 1% and silver surged 2% as investors rotated into traditional havens. Bitcoin, which had already been trading under pressure overnight, hovered near $70,000 and failed to benefit from the rate-cut narrative, underscoring its increasing correlation with risk assets like technology stocks.

Complicating the macroeconomic picture, West Texas Intermediate crude oil surged 6.2% to $86 per barrel, driven by escalating tensions in the Middle East and rising geopolitical risk. Sustained higher energy prices could feed into broader inflation through food and transportation costs, potentially constraining the Fed's ability to cut rates even as the labor market weakens. The combination of softening employment and sticky inflation creates a stagflationary dynamic that central bankers have long sought to avoid.

The February jobs report puts the Federal Reserve in an increasingly difficult position. Chair Jerome Powell and his colleagues must now weigh clear signs of labor market deterioration against the risk that premature easing could reignite inflation — particularly with oil prices climbing and some indicators suggesting parts of the economy may be re-accelerating. Markets will be watching closely for updated guidance at the March 18 meeting, where the central bank's tone could set the trajectory for monetary policy through the rest of the year.

articles.headline: Quantum Scattering Calculations Reveal Resonances in HD Molecular Collisions
articles.subheadline: Full-dimensional simulations of hydrogen deuteride collisions uncover key low-energy resonance features, matching experimental data
articles.body:
A team of physicists has completed what they describe as full-dimensional quantum scattering calculations for collisions between rovibrationally excited hydrogen deuteride (HD) molecules, identifying striking resonance features that align with prior experimental observations and offering new rate coefficients across a wide temperature range.

The research, posted to the arXiv preprint server on March 3 by Bikramaditya Mandal, Hubert Jóźwiak, Piotr Wcisło, and Naduvalath Balakrishnan, tackles one of the more computationally demanding problems in molecular physics: modeling the full quantum dynamics of a four-atom collision system. Using a highly accurate interaction potential for the H₂-H₂ system, the team tracked how HD molecules exchange rotational and vibrational energy when they collide, identifying several near-resonant transitions in which the overall rotational angular momentum is conserved and internal energy is nearly preserved between collision partners.

Among the most notable findings is a strong resonance feature at low collision energies associated with an l=3 partial wave in the incident channel. This shape resonance, occurring at approximately 2.5 kelvin, leaves a clear imprint on the energy dependence of scattering cross sections. The researchers pinpointed the specific anisotropic terms in the interaction potential responsible for driving rotational transitions and determined which angular momentum partial waves contribute to the observed resonant structures. Their computed total cross sections match those from previous experimental measurements, including the resonant features — a significant validation of both the theoretical framework and the potential energy surface employed.

The team also reported rate coefficients for multiple inelastic rotational and ro-vibrational transitions spanning temperatures from 0.1 K to 200 K. These coefficients display a pronounced maximum between 1 K and 10 K, a direct consequence of the l=3 shape resonance. Such data is essential for astrophysical modeling, where HD molecules play a role in the thermal balance of primordial gas clouds and early-universe chemistry, as well as for precision spectroscopy and cold-molecule experiments that probe fundamental physics.

The work sits at the intersection of atomic, chemical, and quantum physics, representing the kind of benchmark calculation that tests the limits of current computational methods. As experimental techniques continue to push molecular collision studies into the ultracold regime, theoretical predictions of this precision become increasingly valuable for interpreting laboratory results and guiding future experiments.

articles.headline: Asia Faces Mounting Fuel Crisis as Shortages Spread Across Region
articles.subheadline: Import-dependent economies from Thailand to India confront diesel queues, price spikes, and supply disruptions amid Persian Gulf uncertainty
articles.body:
A deepening fuel crunch is rippling across Asia, with long queues at filling stations, surging prices, and spot shortages hitting economies that depend heavily on energy imports, according to a Bloomberg report published Friday.

The crisis is striking a broad swath of the continent. In Thailand, farmers are scrambling to secure diesel supplies critical for agricultural operations, while Indian refinery executives are reportedly conducting round-the-clock monitoring of developments in the Persian Gulf — the region that supplies a significant share of Asia's crude oil and refined products. The disruptions underscore the vulnerability of Asian economies, many of which import the vast majority of their fuel needs and have limited strategic reserves to cushion against supply shocks.

The immediate triggers of the crunch remain tied to instability in key Middle Eastern supply routes, though structural factors have compounded the problem. Asia's refining capacity, while growing, has struggled to keep pace with post-pandemic demand recovery in some markets. Countries like India, Japan, South Korea, and much of Southeast Asia route enormous volumes of crude through the Strait of Hormuz, a chokepoint that has long represented a single point of failure for regional energy security. Any perceived threat to those flows tends to send spot prices sharply higher and prompt panic buying downstream.

The economic consequences are potentially severe. Diesel shortages hit particularly hard in agricultural economies where fuel powers irrigation pumps, tractors, and transport fleets that move goods to market. Price hikes at the pump also feed directly into consumer inflation — a politically sensitive issue for governments across the region that have in recent years deployed costly fuel subsidies to keep social unrest at bay. For nations already running fiscal deficits, an extended supply disruption could force difficult choices between subsidizing fuel costs and maintaining broader budget discipline.

Energy analysts will be watching whether the crunch intensifies or eases in the coming weeks, with attention focused on Persian Gulf production decisions and the willingness of major exporters to release additional supply. For now, the episode serves as a stark reminder that Asia's rapid economic growth remains tethered to energy supply chains it does not fully control — a dependence that policymakers have pledged to reduce through renewables and diversification, but that in practice has proved stubbornly resistant to change.

articles.headline: Oil Surges Past $93 as Qatar Warns Gulf Production Could Halt
articles.subheadline: Qatar's energy minister says prices could reach $150 a barrel if Iran conflict persists, raising fears of global economic fallout
articles.body:
Oil prices surged to their highest level in more than two years on Friday after Qatar's energy minister warned that all oil and gas production across the Gulf could cease within days, a scenario that would send shockwaves through the global economy.

Brent crude jumped more than 9% to top $93 per barrel — its highest mark since autumn 2023 — as Saad al-Kaabi, Qatar's energy minister and chief executive of QatarEnergy, told the Financial Times that the widening conflict between the U.S.-Israel coalition and Iran could "bring down the economies of the world." Kaabi said oil could reach $150 per barrel if hostilities persist over the coming weeks, and warned of cascading supply shortages affecting factories and consumers worldwide. QatarEnergy has already halted liquefied natural gas production following military attacks on its facilities, declaring force majeure on its supply contracts. Kaabi said he expects other Gulf exporters to follow suit within days if the conflict continues.

The Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-fifth of the world's daily oil supply typically transits, has seen shipping traffic nearly grind to a halt since the U.S.-Israel war with Iran began last weekend. The chokepoint's effective closure threatens major crude-importing economies including China, India, and Japan. While Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates maintain pipeline alternatives that bypass the strait, analysts warn that prolonged disruption will force Gulf producers to fill storage capacity and ultimately shut in production — a process that could begin within days to weeks depending on available reserves.

The price shock is already reaching consumers. In the United Kingdom, the RAC reported petrol prices rose 3.7 pence per liter and diesel climbed 6 pence since Saturday, both hitting 16-month highs. The UK's Competition and Markets Authority said it is "closely monitoring" fuel pricing. Household energy bills may also increase, though the impact is unlikely to be felt before July given that regulator Ofgem's price cap is already locked in. Jorge Leon, an analyst at Rystad Energy, told the BBC that oil exceeding $100 per barrel is a "realistic scenario" and that the world faces a critical juncture: either a brief, contained energy disruption or the beginning of a major economic crisis.

Investment strategist Lindsay James of Quilter characterized a complete and prolonged halt to Gulf oil and gas production as an "extreme scenario," noting that market positioning suggests investors still expect a relatively swift resolution to Strait of Hormuz disruptions. However, she cautioned that the risk of a more protracted conflict grows with each passing day. Governments would likely release strategic petroleum reserves if prices remain elevated — as occurred following Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022 — but Kaabi warned that even if hostilities ceased immediately, it would take "weeks to months" for production to return to normal levels. The longer the disruption persists, the greater the risk that higher energy costs drag on global GDP growth and reignite inflationary pressures in economies that had only recently brought price increases under control.

articles.headline: China's PLASEN System Achieves First Radioactive Beam Spectroscopy at BRIF
articles.subheadline: Precision laser spectroscopy platform overcomes beam quality challenges to probe exotic nuclei with 100 MHz resolution
articles.body:
A collaboration of more than 40 physicists has fully commissioned a precision laser spectroscopy system at the Beijing Radioactive Ion-beam Facility, marking a significant milestone for China's nuclear physics program. The PLASEN system — short for Precision Laser Spectroscopy for Exotic Nuclei — has now demonstrated its capability to perform high-resolution measurements on radioactive ion beams, opening a new experimental window into the structure of rare isotopes.

The system, detailed in a preprint posted to arXiv on March 4, couples two key instruments: a compact radio-frequency quadrupole cooler-buncher (RFQ-cb) and a collinear resonance ionization spectroscopy setup. The central engineering challenge was taming the large energy spread inherent in the radioactive ion beams produced at BRIF — a problem that can blur spectroscopic measurements beyond usefulness. The RFQ-cb solved this by cooling and compressing the incoming ions into tightly defined bunches, delivering beam quality sufficient for precision work. Using both stable and radioactive rubidium ion beams, the team demonstrated spectral linewidths of approximately 100 MHz and detection efficiency as high as one ion detected per 200 produced.

Those performance figures place PLASEN among the world's competitive collinear laser spectroscopy stations, which are key tools for extracting fundamental nuclear properties such as charge radii, electromagnetic moments, and nuclear spins of isotopes far from stability. Similar facilities operate at CERN's ISOLDE, at TRIUMF in Canada, and at JYFL in Finland, but PLASEN represents the first such fully operational platform in China capable of working with online radioactive beams.

The work was led by Wencong Mei and included collaborators from multiple Chinese institutions as well as international contributor A. Takamine. The authors describe PLASEN as a "state-of-the-art experimental platform" that will support research spanning nuclear, atomic, and molecular physics. With BRIF's ability to produce a range of radioactive species through isotope separation on-line techniques, the facility is positioned to contribute systematic measurements of exotic nuclei across multiple regions of the nuclear chart.

The successful commissioning comes as global investment in radioactive beam facilities continues to accelerate, with major projects under construction or recently completed in South Korea, Japan, and Europe. PLASEN's arrival gives China a domestic capability that previously required beam time at overseas laboratories, potentially accelerating the pace of nuclear structure research from Beijing.

articles.headline: BlackRock Caps Withdrawals From $26 Billion Private Credit Fund
articles.subheadline: Redemption requests hit 9.3% of shares as investor anxiety spreads across the $1.8 trillion private credit industry
articles.body:
BlackRock has restricted investor withdrawals from one of its largest private credit vehicles after redemption requests surged well beyond anticipated levels, signaling growing unease within the fast-expanding private credit sector.

The firm's $26 billion HPS Corporate Lending Fund — one of the biggest non-traded business development companies in the market — disclosed Friday that shareholders sought to redeem 9.3% of their holdings. Management elected to cap repurchases at just 5%, meaning nearly half of the requested withdrawals will go unfulfilled. The gap between what investors wanted out and what BlackRock was willing to return represents a meaningful liquidity constraint for fund participants who expected more flexible access to their capital.

The move arrives at a precarious moment for the $1.8 trillion private credit industry, which has ballooned in recent years as institutional and retail investors alike chased higher yields outside traditional public markets. Private credit funds, which typically lend directly to mid-sized and larger companies, have marketed themselves as attractive alternatives to broadly syndicated loans and high-yield bonds. But the structure of non-traded BDCs — which are not listed on public exchanges — inherently limits liquidity, and the BlackRock episode underscores the tension between investor expectations and the illiquid nature of the underlying assets.

BlackRock acquired HPS Investment Partners in a deal that closed earlier this year, making the fund a centerpiece of the asset manager's push deeper into private markets. The redemption spike now poses a reputational test for that strategy. Gating mechanisms like the one BlackRock employed are standard features built into non-traded fund structures precisely for moments of elevated withdrawal demand, but their activation nonetheless tends to rattle investor confidence and can trigger further redemption pressure in subsequent periods.

The episode echoes similar dynamics that roiled parts of the private real estate market in recent years, when funds managed by Blackstone and Starwood also imposed withdrawal limits amid surging redemption requests. Whether the BlackRock situation remains isolated or marks the beginning of a broader liquidity squeeze across private credit will depend in large part on macroeconomic conditions and whether credit quality in the underlying loan portfolios holds up under pressure from elevated interest rates and a slowing economy.

articles.headline: New Method Makes Curvature-Aware Molecular Simulations 24x Faster
articles.subheadline: Projected Hessian Learning sidesteps a major computational bottleneck in training machine-learning interatomic potentials
articles.body:
A team of researchers has developed a training framework that captures the rich curvature information encoded in a molecule's potential energy surface — without the prohibitive computational cost that has long made such approaches impractical. The method, called Projected Hessian Learning (PHL), achieves accuracy on par with full second-order training while delivering more than 24-fold speedups per training epoch.

The work, posted to the arXiv preprint server on March 4 by Austin Rodriguez, Justin S. Smith, Sakib Matin, Nicholas Lubbers, Kipton Barros, and Jose L. Mendoza-Cortes, addresses a fundamental scaling problem in the construction of machine-learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs). These potentials are surrogate models trained on quantum-mechanical calculations to predict molecular energies and forces at a fraction of the cost. Incorporating Hessian matrices — the second derivatives of energy that describe how forces change with atomic positions — dramatically improves model accuracy, particularly for reactive chemistry and transition states. But explicitly computing, storing, and training on full Hessians scales quadratically with system size, making the approach untenable for large or complex molecules.

PHL circumvents this bottleneck by never constructing the full Hessian matrix. Instead, it projects curvature information along stochastic probe directions using Hessian-vector products, a computation that scales linearly rather than quadratically with the number of atoms. The method employs an unbiased stochastic trace-based loss function, ensuring that the essential second-order information is faithfully captured without quadratic memory growth. The researchers benchmarked PHL on a chemically diverse dataset comprising reactants, products, transition states, intrinsic reaction coordinates, and normal-mode sampled geometries, all computed at the ωB97XD/6-31G(d) level of quantum chemistry theory.

The results showed that both Hessian-vector product training schemes — using either one-hot or randomized probe directions — matched the energy, force, and Hessian accuracy of full Hessian training. Randomized projections proved especially advantageous for geometries far from equilibrium, such as transition states, consistently outperforming fixed one-column probing when computational budgets allowed only a single Hessian-vector product per molecule per minibatch.

The advance could have significant practical implications for computational chemistry and materials science, where MLIPs are increasingly used to simulate chemical reactions, phase transitions, and molecular dynamics at scales inaccessible to direct quantum-mechanical calculation. By reducing curvature-informed training to the same computational complexity as force training, PHL opens the door to building more accurate potentials for larger and more chemically complex systems — a persistent challenge in the field.

articles.headline: U.S. Employers Cut 92,000 Jobs in February in Surprise Decline
articles.subheadline: One of the largest payroll drops since the pandemic raises fresh doubts about labor market stability
articles.body:
The U.S. labor market delivered a jarring setback in February, with nonfarm payrolls falling by 92,000 — one of the steepest monthly declines since the pandemic-era disruptions — and the unemployment rate ticking higher, according to new government data that caught economists off guard.

The decline followed what had been a strong start to 2026, and it undercuts the prevailing narrative among Wall Street forecasters and Federal Reserve officials that the job market was finding its footing after what had been the weakest year for hiring outside of a recession in decades. While analysts had anticipated some drag from a healthcare workers' strike and severe weather in parts of the country, the breadth of the pullback extended well beyond those temporary factors, with a wide array of industries shedding positions.

The report lands at a sensitive moment for monetary policymakers. The Fed has been weighing the balance between persistent inflation pressures and signs of cooling economic activity, and a deteriorating employment picture could accelerate the timeline for rate cuts that markets have been pricing in for later this year. A labor market that appeared to be stabilizing gave officials room to hold rates steady, but sustained weakness in hiring would shift that calculus considerably.

For businesses and consumers, the data raises uncomfortable questions about the durability of the economic expansion. Consumer spending — the engine of U.S. growth — depends heavily on steady job creation and wage gains. A single month of weak payrolls does not necessarily signal a recession, but the scale of February's decline, combined with last year's already subdued hiring trends, suggests the labor market may be more fragile than headline economic indicators had implied.

Economists will now scrutinize upcoming data releases, including jobless claims and the next payrolls report, to determine whether February was an aberration driven by one-off disruptions or the beginning of a more troubling downward trend. The answer will likely shape both Fed policy and market expectations for the months ahead.

articles.headline: Markets Rocked as War Fears, Weak Jobs Data, and Credit Anxiety Converge
articles.subheadline: Oil prices surge on Middle East escalation while equities slide amid a triple threat of bearish signals
articles.body:
Global markets came under intense pressure as a confluence of geopolitical, economic, and financial risks sent investors scrambling for safety, pushing oil prices sharply higher while dragging equities and risk assets lower.

A weaker-than-expected jobs report compounded fears that the U.S. economy may be losing momentum at a particularly precarious moment. The soft employment data landed just as an escalating military conflict in the Middle East drove crude oil prices upward, reviving concerns about persistent inflation that could constrain the Federal Reserve's ability to cut interest rates. The combination rattled equity markets across regions, with stocks falling broadly as traders reassessed the growth outlook.

Adding to the bearish mood, mounting anxiety over the private-credit industry further eroded risk appetite. The sector, which has ballooned in recent years as banks retreated from certain lending activities, has drawn increasing scrutiny from regulators and investors worried about opacity, valuations, and potential contagion effects in a slowing economy. Those concerns appeared to weigh heavily on financial sentiment, contributing to a broader flight from riskier assets.

Bitcoin, often treated as a barometer of speculative appetite, tumbled as the risk-off tone deepened. The cryptocurrency's decline underscored the breadth of the selloff, which cut across asset classes and geographies. Oil's sharp rally, meanwhile, reflected growing fears that the widening Middle East conflict could disrupt supply chains in a region critical to global energy markets — a scenario that would further complicate central banks' efforts to bring inflation sustainably back to target.

The convergence of these pressures presents a challenging environment for policymakers and investors alike.
